english: Stir-Fried Udon with Shrimp and Crabstick

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for fried shrimp udon; mapped to the closest parent class 'udon noodle dish' (FOODON_03315671) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — stir-fried, wheat-based noodles with seafood
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as closest defensible category; no specific code for yaki udon with shrimp exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of fried shrimp udon weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant or hawker portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodle dishes, stir-fried, with seafood; composite estimate derived from udon noodle (cooked), shrimp (cooked), imitation crab, and stir-fry sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for fried shrimp udon as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries: cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 168917), cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), imitation crab (FDC ID 175168), and stir-fry sauce components (soy sauce, mirin, vegetable oil). The input record's per_100g values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 7.0 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite estimate and are retained. Micronutrient values are similarly consistent with USDA component data and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles, boiled (GI 55, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried shrimp udon as a composite dish. GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles, boiled (GI 55, glucose scale), as published in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (0–55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Stir-frying with oil and protein (shrimp, crabstick) may modestly lower the effective GI further. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 80.0) / 100 = 44.0 (carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 20.0 × 4 = 80.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g from soy sauce, dashi, mirin, and processed imitation crabstick
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of 450 mg/100 g (1800 mg per standard 400 g serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; substituting low-sodium soy sauce and dashi and replacing imitation crabstick with fresh shrimp can meaningfully reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined udon noodles at the upper boundary of low GI (GI 55) with low dietary fibre (1.25 g/100 g) and added sugars from mirin, producing a high glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should consume this dish in small portions due to its high glycaemic load (GL 44 per 400 g serving) driven by refined wheat udon noodles and mirin-derived sugars; increasing vegetable content and reducing noodle proportion will lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (450 mg/100 g), cholesterol (45 mg/100 g), and moderate fat (6.25 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its high sodium content and moderate cholesterol level; the relatively low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) is a mitigating factor, but the sodium load from processed ingredients poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Shrimp Udon (エビ焼きうどん, Ebi Yaki Udon) is a Japanese stir-fried noodle dish featuring thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles cooked in a hot wok or pan with succulent shrimp and imitation crabstick (surimi), seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and dashi stock. The dish is a popular comfort food in Japan and widely available across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it appears on Japanese restaurant and hawker menus. The noodles are pre-boiled and then stir-fried at high heat to develop a light char and absorb the savoury-sweet sauce. Vegetables such as cabbage, carrot, and spring onion are commonly added. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 7.0 g protein, 6.25 g fat, and 20.0 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 450 mg. The glycaemic index is approximately 55 (low GI upper boundary per University of Sydney classification), yielding a high glycaemic load of 44 per standard 400 g serving. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium from soy sauce, dashi, and processed crabstick; high concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate load and low fibre; and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to sodium and cholesterol. Allergens include wheat (gluten), crustaceans, fish (pollock in crabstick), and soy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of shrimp udon?
Is fried udon high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for fried shrimp udon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (udon noodles, shrimp, imitation crab, stir-fry sauce) and are consistent with the input record's values, which are retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, udon noodles boiled, GI 55). Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried udon dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 4. Input record name 'Fried Shirmp Udon' contains a typographical error ('Shirmp'); corrected to 'Fried Shrimp Udon' as the canonical English food name. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (udon noodles, boiled); no direct GI measurement for stir-fried shrimp udon composite exists in approved sources. Per the University of Sydney GI Database standard classification, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI category (0–55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. The validator feedback from iteration 2 flagged gi_category 'medium' as inconsistent with GI 55; this has been corrected to 'low' in this iteration. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315671 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish. 7. Halal status is marked false as imitation crabstick and mirin (alcohol-containing) are common ingredients; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed.
